## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hey, how are you doing? So I want to comment a bit on this latest announcement that Meta is going to shut the doors on Facebook podcasting. I think there's a misunderstanding about how Facebook podcasting worked based on some of the comments I've received about it so far. So I kind of wanted to take a step back and explain why it means something to me that's going away. So this has only been around for 10 months officially and actually it's really only been tested. So basically if you have a pack, so no one is building a podcast entirely in Facebook. It doesn't really make sense the way it works, right? So I think the thought by some people is that you're recording using Facebook's tools and publishing to an audience on Facebook. That's not what it's happening. There are some bite-sized audio clips and stuff like that that you can do on Facebook. Those people are getting screwed. But otherwise all this is connecting your RSS feed from your show that you already have to Facebook. So it creates a section on your Facebook page for your podcast to hold all your episodes that you already have been recording and published everywhere else anyway. And every time you publish a new episode it goes to the news feed of people in the US on mobile only. So it's just a small percentage of people potentially, right? So that's all it was. So it really was no investment for anyone to do this. For me, it kind of opened my eyes to what was possible on the platform. So back in October I connected my show to Facebook and I immediately saw the potential. Now I saw there's an initial kind of burst I think when I did it. There's no built-in way to promote your show, your podcast on Facebook. But I found a workaround. And so that helped a little bit. But it's about 4% of my downloads in 2022 come from Facebook. So not huge, but at the same time if your traffic dropped by 4% or if your revenue dropped by 4%, you care, right? So I see a lot of comments about don't build your house on Reddit and land, blah, blah, blah. Well, all of these destinations are rented, right? So whether it's Apple Podcasts or Spotify or Stitch or any of those, they're just all options. These are all places my podcast has been going to and now one of them's disappearing. So this is why it bothers me though, right? Because of the potential that was never realized that I wish Metta would have at least tried.

## [03:03 - 05:49]

The fact that it only went to US, never expanded beyond the US. So we never really got to see how much of an impact it could make. Because I knew every time my show published to Facebook that it would get limited run because it's only going to be people in the US, only people on mobile at the time. And algorithm evolved too. So that was the first thing. But also it's like all these potential features. It's funny, a little ironic. Yesterday was the day that I also published a podcast episode about the features that Metta needs for Facebook podcasting. Once you know it, same day that it's announced it's shut down. But there's so many things that could have been done. Like you could have run ads, which was never made possible, just a workaround, to promote each individual episode, to promote your show, right? You could focus on getting new subscribers. You could target people who are your subscribers. You could target people who aren't, try to get new subscribers. You could target people based on those who listened to a specific episode on Facebook. Or how much they've listened on Facebook. Or your most engaged listeners on Facebook. Because not everybody's a podcast listener. So it's kind of, it's like all these things are like, oh man, this will be awesome. And we'll never see it happen. So to me, it's not that I lost something. 4%, 4% downloads. Facebook never provided any data whatsoever on like how much people were listening or anything like that. I don't know how many subscribers I had. Nothing. So all that was whatever. I didn't know that anyway. The only thing I know from Libsyn is that it's about 4% of my downloads. Downloads don't really mean a whole lot. You want to engage listeners. So to me, it's really about the potential. And that's what bothers me most. But what seems to be happening is it's a redirection of resources towards all this metaverse stuff. Metaverse, the TikTok, short form video, all that business, all this stuff. I'm not really all that into, and I guess I need to kind of figure it out. So those are my thoughts. I understand it's a niche thing. And unless you're in the US, unless you have a podcast, it probably doesn't really impact you, but it might impact me. And that's my story. So I'd like to hear what you think about it too. Let me know. Thanks.
